question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 40416/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any audits that have been carried out on the quality of repairs to the road network.
answer_text: My officials inspect and make-safe defects by working to address the highest priority defects as fast as possible, in accordance with our policy to ensure the safety of the travelling public.Defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor. On completion of the repair, my officials undertake a percentage check to assess the quality of the pothole repair and confirm works for payment if the repair meets the required threshold. If a repair fails to satisfy the required standards, officials will request that it be rectified and subject it to further inspection. This process will continue until the repair fully complies with all stipulated requirements.These checks are undertaken on both external contractors and our internal contractor workforce.More generally, the Department’s routine road safety inspection regime helps identify defects with inspections scheduled at 1, 3 or 6 month frequency depending on traffic volumes, and additional inspections carried out to investigate customer enquiries or complaints.The Department also undertakes oversight in relation to utility reinstatements, where there is a prescribed specification for materials and workmanship and a minimum guarantee period of two years (and three years for deep excavations). The Department has powers to carry out investigatory works and to undertake followup inspections until any defective reinstatement has been remedied.Taken together, these inspections and checks provide the Department’s practical audit trail for repair quality - both for works completed by contractors and for reinstatements associated with utility openings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41437/22-27, and as her Department co-chairs the group, to detail all the meetings her officials attended of the Community Resilience Group, over each of the last five years, with (i) Garden Village, Ballycraigy; (ii) Abbey View; (iii) Meadowside; and (iv) Masserine and Riverside, Antrim.
answer_text: The Regional Community Resilience Group, co-chaired by my Department and Local Government, aims to work on a multi-agency partnership basis to support communities at risk of flooding. There are over 50 groups in the North including several in the Antrim area. As previously advised responsibility for routine annual engagement and maintenance of group membership rests with the relevant council.My Department remains fully committed to this partnership approach and continues to play an active and supportive role with these RCRG groups through texts and emails, particularly in advance of and during periods of severe weather. Officials met with representatives of the Riverside, Masserene and Meadowside communities on 4 February and 13 March 2026 following the flooding from Storm Chandra with a further meeting to be convened over the coming weeks. Although officials have not met the groups individually since they were initially established.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 40961/22-27, AQW 40962/22-27 and AQW 40963/22-27, to detail (i) the reasons for the five year delay in implementing the weight restriction on Craigarogan Road and (ii) the current stage of the legislative process for the weight restriction.
answer_text: My Department has faced significant resource challenges in the recruitment and retention of staff over recent years, especially in relation to specialist roles, which have impacted our ability to deliver essential services and infrastructure projects. These resourcing pressures have, in turn, impacted the pace at which the weight restriction on Craigarogan Road has progressed.However, I am pleased to advise that the draft order is expected to be published during week commencing 30 March 2026, with the legislative process, including consideration by the NI Assembly expected to be completed by June 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) short-term; and (ii) long-term, plans for the Muckamore Abbey site, particularly for the unused or unutilised buildings.
answer_text: The Department is currently considering the future use of the Muckamore Abbey site in the context of both immediate and longer-term strategic health and social care needs and funding availability. This includes assessing best potential use of unused and underutilised buildings to ensure value for money and optimal service delivery across the system. Health Estates officials have undertaken a review of the estate and will engage with relevant stakeholders to determine if any of the underutilised assets can be repurposed to meet health needs in the short to longer term.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount her Department is spending on the Narrow Water Bridge project.
answer_text: Narrow Water Bridge is a major investment in landmark cross border infrastructure and will provide for car, cycle and pedestrian traffic, connecting the North and South of Ireland.The Irish Government approved Shared Island funding for the total cost of the project based on the successful tender price of €102m (plus VAT).The project is being led by the Irish Government and managed by Louth County who will also be responsible for the bridge when complete, which will include maintenance and the ‘lifting’ operation.My Department is not funding this project, however my officials continue to be engaged with the Irish Government and Louth County Council during the construction phase and are involved in the Project Oversight Group to assist in the successful delivery of the project and connection with the road network in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount her Department has spent on consultation fees on footpaths for active travel, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Active travel capital projects typically include a combination of footpaths, segregated cycling facilities and/or shared pedestrian and cycling facilities. Spend is recorded by project rather than by specific provisions, therefore a breakdown of spend for footpaths alone is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average cost to her Department to resurface one mile of road.
answer_text: My Department maintains a large and varied road network of both rural and urban roads, ranging from low trafficked routes to high speed ‘A’ Class roads and Dual Carriageways. The costs per mile can vary widely depending on a range of factors, including the depth of resurfacing required (a factor of traffic volume and road condition); the type of resurfacing material specified (a factor of traffic volume and road condition);  road width;  whether additional drainage is required; the nature and extent of the traffic control and diversions; and whether there are restrictions on the periods of work due to concerns around traffic congestion. Given the wide range of variables, an average cost to the Department to resurface one mile of road is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of road defects reported in South Antrim, over the last year.
answer_text: My Department identifies actionable defects for repair through routine highway safety inspections and through public reporting via the online Report a Fault system.Actionable defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor.  On completion of the repair, inspections are undertaken on a percentage of completed road defect repairs by external contractors before payments are processed. Checks on the work of the Department’s internal contractor are also undertaken.Officials use DEM 160/21 Road Maintenance Standards for Safety – Limited Service when determining actionable defects and the Manual of Contract Documents for Highways Works when determining a repair to be satisfactory. During the period, 1 January 2025 to 31 December 2025, there have been a total of 3,046 actionable defects recorded within the Antrim and Newtownabbey area.

To ask the Minister of Health, following recent local media reports, to detail any action that will be taken to secure all areas of the Muckamore Abbey Hospital site to prevent further trespassing incidents taking place.
answer_text: As the owner of the Muckamore Abbey Hospital site, the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is responsible for security of the site.   The Trust have confirmed that the following measures are in place to mitigate the risk of future trespassing incidents taking place:Since 30th November 2025 Security staff presence 24/7 to manage barriers, reception and site security;Daily site walk through of main site, site across the road from main site, vacant buildings, fenced site adjacent to main site vacant buildings, approximately 4 occasions per day by security staff;		CCTV site coverage utilising a number of existing cameras, relocated to the exterior of buildings as they become vacant, to provide external coverage;Use of a CCTV central viewing station, providing site coverage from a network of internal & external cameras;Intruder alarms installed to ‘mothballed’ (unoccupied) buildings complete with Digi-Air communication installed – this will alert an external central resourced station of an activation, which is then relayed to Site key holder &/or Security;CCTV & warning signage placed throughout site;Barriers installed at front entrance, access via swipe card for staff, intercom for others, managed from reception by security staff;Vacant site areas monitored at night from a safe distance by security; andVacant buildings are secured via windows and doorways boarded or built up with cement blocks, to prevent access.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, following the approval given to the Department of Health to overspend its budget by £100million to award pay parity, whether he will approve other Departments facing financial pressures to do the same.
answer_text: The Executive collectively agreed to the Health Minister’s Direction to delivery pay parity for health service staff. It is important to set out the Health Minister has agreed to any funding shortfall in his department at the end of 2025-26 being deducted from the Health opening budget for 2026-27. The Health Minister has assured the Executive he has a plan in place to reset the Health and Social Care system budget and has indicated he will continue to do everything he can to further reduce his departments funding gap.Any similar decisions would require full Executive agreement, not approval by the Finance Minister alone.I have made it consistently clear that all departments must live within their budget allocations. Departments should have already been taking actions to ensure they live within the budget agreed earlier this year. Efforts must now be redoubled.I have reiterated to my Ministerial colleagues the severity of the constrained financial situation and the need to work in partnership, to manage the extremely challenging financial position. All Ministers must play their part.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of reports that PSNI officers have been asked to remove firearms before meeting Sinn Féin representatives. [R]
answer_text: Policies and procedures relating to firearms are an operational matter for the PSNI which is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, of which you are a Member. I understand from reports that you had the opportunity to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable, who set out his assessment and position unequivocally. As the facts of these reports are now in dispute, the Policing Board is the most appropriate forum in which to investigate further and establish their veracity or otherwise.That fact notwithstanding, I believe any request for PSNI officers to surrender their personal protection weapons while on duty would not only be entirely inappropriate but also pose a significant risk to the personal safety of officers and public safety more widely.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any plans she has to assist the Police Service of Northern Ireland with its shortfall in funding for the 2025-26 financial year.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether public petitions are given any weight in the decision to install a pedestrian crossing, particularly when the number of pedestrians has been deemed as too low to be eligible.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need. Regrettably, when assessed against these competing priorities, there are some schemes that do not score highly enough for inclusion in a works programme given levels of funding available. My Department’s procedure for the assessment of locations for controlled crossings is outlined in RSPPG E005 Controlled Crossings: Assessment Procedures.Assessments are kept on a live system and scorings may be reconsidered as a result of changes to the environment at a location such as increased housing development.

answer_text: ANSWEREarlier this year a scoping study was completed on the issue of shared property management and a report was submitted to me for my consideration.I have examined the report and accept that reform is desirable in this area. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any conversations he has had with the Minister of Finance regarding the regulation of property management companies in respect of housing policy, since taking up office.
answer_text: At a meeting on 2 October 2024 the Finance Minister noted her desire to resume work on the issue of managing agents when resourced to do so. I agreed that my officials would liaise with colleagues in her Department to inform their work.  My officials subsequently engaged with Department of Finance colleagues on two separate occasions to feed into this work. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the conversations she has had with the Minister of Finance, since taking office, regarding the regulation of property management companies in respect of consumer protection and company law.
answer_text: My Department has no remit regarding the regulation of property management companies, beyond the interest of the Trading Standards Service, which is solely from a consumer protection perspective and is covered under existing consumer protection legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time for a red flagged appointment through the new Single Regional Waiting list for breast assessments.
answer_text: Due to the necessary validation processes required to ensure the accuracy and completeness of the data, there is an inherent time lag in reporting. The next Cancer Waiting Time Statistics bulletin, covering the period from 1 July to 30 September 2025, is scheduled for publication on 15 January 2026.  The most recent figures have a reporting date of 30 June 2025 and are available on the Department’s website at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-times The Department is however aware that the current regional waiting time for red flag breast assessment across the region is unacceptable.  My Department is working on plans to reduce waiting times in the short term with additional waiting list initiatives clinics and utilising Independent Sector capacity and in the long term with an additional £5m investment in core capacity of the HSC Trusts.This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the rationale for seeking to extend penalty notice arrangements to public order related offences, including riotous behaviour.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department’s plans for increasing the number of adult disability respite facilities.
answer_text: My Department is leading on the reform of Adult Social Care through the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The ‘Maximising Capacity Workstream’ of the Forum will support expansion of placement provision within care home settings from a residential and nursing care perspective. This will enhance traditional models of respite/short break provision.In addition to these traditional bed-based services, my Department is also progressing the Shared Lives Model, to further enhance access to day support and short break services in Shared Lives Carer’s homes. Shared Lives is a fully regulated service which offers an alternative and highly flexible form of accommodation and care support. Shared Lives is based around Shared Lives Carers sharing their home, family and community life with another person who needs support.  The development of Shared Lives will offer additional choice for older people and adults with a learning disability and their families and carers, regarding access to short breaks. In respect of Adult Learning Disability Services, my Department launched the Learning Disability Service Model for Public Consultation on 19 August 2025. The Learning Disability Service Model sets out the Department of Health’s vision in respect of the following themes; Home, Transitions, Health & Wellbeing, Meaningful Lives & Citizenship, Families & Carers (including plans regarding respite/ short breaks), and Mental Health & Behavioural Support. It is considered that the Public Consultation will enable adults with a learning disability, their carers and families to shape service delivery regionally, across Adult Learning Disability Services. My Department remains fully committed to actively working with Trusts and other agencies to enhance short break provision for adults with a physical or learning disability and to offer support to their families/carers in their caring role, in accordance with assessed need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the number of males entering the teaching profession.
answer_text: My assessment is that there are not enough male teachers in the workforce or choosing teaching as a profession. I consider that it is important for pupils to have male teachers as role models to help break down the stereotype of teaching being a predominately female profession.Each year my Department produces a statistical bulletin to provide analysis of the latest annual data collections relating to teacher numbers and pupil: teacher ratios (PTRs) in grant-aided schools.  From the 2024/25 academic year bulletin:77% of all teachers are female and 23% are male in 2024/25.The teacher headcount is 21,803 which includes 5,010 male teachers.There are no male teachers in nursery schools.In primary and preparatory schools 15.6% of teachers are male.In post primary schools 30.1% of teachers are male.In special schools 19.9% of teachers are male.The percentage of male principals in special schools is 30.8%.The percentage of male vice principals in primary and preparatory schools is 29.8%.When I consider leadership positions;In special schools 30.8% of principals are male.In primary and preparatory schools 29.8% of vice principals are male. In nursery, primary and preparatory schools 36.7% of principals and 29.8% of vice principals are male.In post-primary schools 51.1% of principals and 43.3% of vice principals are male.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of (i) arrests; (ii) charges; and (iii) subsequent convictions in relation to the destruction of 5G masts, since January 2022 to date.
answer_text: In relation to parts (i) and (ii) of the question, the Department does not hold information on persons arrested or charged by police in its datasets.  The recording of information on the number of persons arrested or charged for an offence by the police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to persons arrested or charged by the police for an offence relating to the destruction of 5G masts, as detailed in your question, to the PSNI.In relation to part (iii) of the question, there is no specific offence relation to the destruction of a 5G mast.  Such offences may be prosecuted under more generic criminal damage offence descriptions. Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not contain information on the nature of any item damaged or destroyed when such an offence occurred.  Therefore, from information held in Departmental datasets, it is not possible to identify those convicted of an offence directly related to the destruction of 5G masts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the estimated damage to the economy due to the reported attacks on 5G masts from January 2022 to date.
answer_text: I condemn the wanton destruction of this essential communications infrastructure; it is despicable and has serious consequences for local communities, businesses and public services. I am aware that the mast attacks are under investigation by the PSNI, and I would urge anyone with any information to pass it on to the policeMobile Network Operators (MNOs) have stated that attacks on masts have caused a drop in service levels in affected areas, but mast optimisation has been performed. When mast sites are taken out of service it creates two linked problems: lack of coverage and related lack of capacity. To help with both, operators can complete ‘optimisation’ in the area with changes on surrounding sites to compensate for the coverage as much as possible. This will maintain service but not necessarily to the desired level so data heavy activities may be impacted. It is therefore not possible to estimate the damage to the economy due to many variables. Industry advise that the cost of these attacks could run to millions of pounds. These attacks may also inhibit future investment from communication providers. The Barrier Busting Taskforce Communication Subgroup is developing a programme of information regarding mobile technologies, including around perceived public health concerns which may be motivating attacks on masts.I am scheduled to meet with representatives of the mobile industry later this month, the mast attacks will be discussed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail an update on the progress of the report into the economic and social benefits of marching bands.
answer_text: At my instruction, my officials completed a high-level internal review of the health of the Ulster-Scots/Ulster-British marching band sector which I received in March this year for my information. I asked that the review consider two previous reports on the same topic, as conducted by the Department for Culture, Arts and Leisure (DCAL) in 2010/11 and RSM McClure Watters in 2013, with a view to determining if there had been any change in the approximate number of currently active marching bands; number of band members; or the local economic impact of marching bands. I also sought advice on whether a more substantial review may be merited.Following engagement with six umbrella organisations, such as marching band forums, that represented 275 marching bands in total, it was possible to canvas a broad numerical and geographical range of bands. All but one of the organisations stated their numbers (individuals and bands) had remained steady or increased in the preceding 5-10 year period. The review speculated that the decline (10%) in the other organisation’s figures was due to individuals from smaller bands gravitating towards larger bands with some smaller bands consequently ceasing operations. In examining the underlying data, the review found no significant changes in the number of bands or band members since the two previous reports were produced in 2010/11 and 2013. It also noted that the number of parades has increased over the last 40 years, whilst the number of PSNI-designated “sensitive” parades had decreased over the last 12 years. The review echoed both previous reports in highlighting the challenge of obtaining economic data but speculated that the economic impact of marching bands appeared similarly unchanged.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any steps her Department is taking to attract high-quality, long-term investment to Northern Ireland, in particular South Antrim.
answer_text: The Communities in Transition (CIT) programme has delivered £24 million of support to build community resilience against paramilitarism, criminality and coercive control across eight areas up to March 2025. A total of £1.59 million has been allocated to North Belfast.  As part of the third phase of the programme, which started in May, 35 contracts totalling £2.9 million were awarded, including in the New Lodge and Ardoyne areas of North Belfast. North Belfast has also received funding of £280,000 this year to deliver programmes that focus on issues such as Mental Health and Addictions, Raising the Aspirations of Young People, Addressing Exploitation, and Addressing the Impact of Paramilitarism on the Physical Landscape.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any financial support her Department has provided to Belfast International Airport in the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department and its partner organisations are fully aware of the critical role that our airports play in the delivery of our Economic Priorities through connecting us to the rest of the world, and in opening us up to business, tourism and education.In Financial Year 24/25, Tourism NI supported Belfast International Airport to a value of £44,490 for media buying services at the airport.For current Financial Year 25/26, Tourism NI confirmed that agreement is in place with the airport in principle for further media buying services at £88,200. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether patients are able to choose what Health and Social Care Trust they receive hospital care from, regardless of their home address.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, patients typically receive hospital care from the Health and Social Care Trust local to their home address. However, there are exceptions, such as:- Specialized Care: Patients might be referred to a hospital or service outside their local Trust area for specialized treatments.- Emergency Care: Patients can access emergency care at any hospital without prior approval or referral.Patients attending an Emergency Department (ED) by ambulance or as a walk-in will be assessed and treated as appropriate, regardless of their Trust of residence.  Patients conveyed by ambulance will be taken to the closest ED, unless a bypass protocol determines clinical care should be provided at an alternative location, for example at a regional specialty service.  At times of increased pressure, NIAS may convey patients to an alternative hospital if appropriate.  With the exception of a small number of areas, GPs are entitled to refer to which ever Health and Social Care Trust they feel is appropriate for their patient.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the criteria for triggering a Serious Adverse Incident relating to an individual’s care in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is a requirement on all Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts to routinely report Serious Adverse Incidents (SAIs) to my Department in line with the Procedure for the reporting and follow up of SAIs (the procedure).The Procedure provides guidance to HSC organisations and Special Agencies (SA) in relation to the reporting and follow up of SAIs arising during the course of their business or commissioned service and sets out the definition and criteria of an SAI as follows:Definition of an Adverse Incident:‘Any event or circumstances that could have or did lead to harm, loss or damage to people, property, environment or reputation’ arising during the course of the business of a HSC organisation / Special Agency or commissioned service.The following criteria will determine whether or not an adverse incident constitutes an SAI, the incident must meet one or more of the criteria:•	serious injury to, or the unexpected/unexplained death of:                  -	a service user, (including a Looked After Child (LAC) or a child whose name is on the Child Protection Register and those events which should be reviewed through a significant event audit)-	a staff member in the course of their work-	a member of the public whilst visiting a HSC facility;•	unexpected serious risk to a service user and/or staff member and/or member of the public;•	unexpected or significant threat to provide service and/or maintain business continuity;•	serious self-harm or serious assault (including attempted suicide, homicide and sexual assaults) by a service user, a member of staff or a member of the public within any healthcare facility providing a commissioned service;•	serious self-harm or serious assault (including homicide and sexual assaults) -	on other service users, -	on staff or, -	on members of the publicby a service user in the community who has a mental illness or disorder (as defined within the Mental Health (NI) Order 1986) and/or known to/referred to mental health and related services (including CAMHS, psychiatry of old age or leaving and aftercare services) and/or learning disability services, in the 12 months prior to the incident; •	suspected suicide of a service user who has a mental illness or disorder (as defined within the Mental Health (NI) Order 1986) and/or known to/referred to mental health and related services (including CAMHS, psychiatry of old age or leaving and aftercare services) and/or learning disability services, in the 12 months prior to the incident;•	serious incidents of public interest or concern relating to:-	any of the criteria above -	theft, fraud, information breaches or data losses -	a member of HSC staff or independent practitioner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how many occasions staff returning to schools have been supported by Education Authority reintegration support, in each of the last 10 years, and broken down by the length of time.
answer_text: The information requested is not held by the Department.Officials have requested the information from the Education Authority (EA) and their reply indicates the following:The Education Authority does not have a universal approach for documenting and reporting on the specific arrangements put in place for staff who return from periods of long absence from work, whether it be family friendly arrangements, precautionary suspension, career break or sickness absence. This detail is managed locally by school leaders and line managers and support and guidance is sought from relevant teams specific to each scenario/context i.e. Health and Wellbeing, HR, Organisational Development, School Development etc.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how the Northern Ireland Housing Executive plan to accommodate tenants who have electric vehicles and require charging points on their homes, particularly those living in flats or those in properties that do not front a road.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘The Electric Vehicle Charging Policy developed by the Housing Executive relates only to instances in which Housing Executive tenants with in-curtilage parking wish to install EV charge points at their homes. The policy does not permit the installation of these by tenants in any other circumstances - for example on-street or communal parking areas - as this would involve trailing leads that would represent a trip hazard and health and safety risk to pedestrians.  At present the Housing Executive has no strategy, or indeed funding, for it to install EV charge points in its estates.  However, we are open to working with others in projects to increase the availability of on-street charge points in residential streets where off-street parking is not available.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to review the decision to not allow Policing and Community Safety Partnership funding to be used to procure further speed indicator devices.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 12 - AQO 2075/22-27			ANSWERMy Department alongside Northern Ireland Policing Board, via a PCSP Joint Committee, provides annual operational and administration funding of £4.25m to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) to identify and help address local community safety issues in each council area, including road safety. I am aware that PCSPs have previously purchased speed indicator devices (SIDs) to help address the issue of road safety in their local area, and that there are currently 159 mobile and fixed SIDs located throughout Northern Ireland that have been procured / are being maintained by PCSPs. The Department for Infrastructure (DfI) is policy lead for road safety and, while it does not usually fund SIDs, it has provided a memorandum on the DfI website which details the use of these.  Given the number of initiatives in place across Northern Ireland to help address road safety, and the need to balance this with tackling other equally as important community safety issues in local areas with a limited amount of funding, a decision was taken by Joint Committee that, while PCSP funding can still be utilised to maintain existing SIDs, no further SIDs should be procured within 2025/26. It has been agreed that a review will be carried out on SIDs by the end of the year, with input from PSNI and DfI. In the interim, I am also aware that PSNI, DfI, PCSPs and Joint Committee are meeting in the summer to discuss current arrangements with the various partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the clean-up operation following Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Storm Éowyn brought winds of more than 90mph to the North, with a Red Wind warning from 0700am on Friday 24th January until 1400 Hours on Friday.My Department had 3040 incidents affecting Roads, including 2700 obstructions of fallen trees, branches, and utility poles affecting the road network.At that time the priority was to reopen roads and support our vital services. My Department’s Road Divisions continue to clear of debris from road sides but this must be programmed and prioritised with other on-going safety related maintenance work that must be completed. As such, the clear up work will continue for some time.Two road closures associated with storm damage are currently in place within the Causeway Coast & Glens area.The U5803 Carrickmore Road in Ballycastle, is severely damaged due to a combination of historic tidal erosion and further erosion emanating from the storm. Whilst the road surface is intact, it is closed for public safety reasons. However, I am pleased to announce that work has just commenced to have this repaired and reopened.A section of road on Rathlin Island also remains closed. Work on a new retaining wall design and repair is being developed to support the road and my Department will progress this as quickly as possible.While there remains some material on verges at a limited number of sites across the road network, none of this is considered to be a danger to road safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the number of Housing Executive properties in South Antrim that have been vacant for 12 months or more.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the roll-out of TransformED NI.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he will take to ensure construction of the Birch Hill Mental Health Centre facility at Bush Road, Antrim, can resume as soon as possible.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline the number of children that have been treated for the effects of glycerol consumption in the last five years.
answer_text: I am aware that there have been recent media reports into the issue of glycerol consumption, or glycerol intoxication as it is known. Glycerol is a common additive that is used in ‘slush ice’ drinks and when it is consumed, particularly amongst young children, glycerol intoxication can occur on occasions.  As a result of this the Food Standards Agency has guidance on the consumption of such products amongst younger children.According to a recent report published by academics in University College Dublin 21 young children in the UK and Ireland have been hospitalised after consuming drinks containing glycerol over the last six years.  These cases involved children aged between 2 and 6 years old who experience severe symptoms such as unconsciousness and symptoms linked to hypoglycaemia or low blood sugar within an hour of consumption.  Fortunately, most children, if they do have symptoms, will have mild symptoms.  These include headaches, nausea and vomiting.My officials have checked and in Northern Ireland we do not have specific data codes in our systems that record ‘glycerol intoxication’ in hospitals so therefore I am not able to highlight specific figures for Northern Ireland.Whilst specific data for Northern Ireland is not available, the Food Standards Agency has reported two cases in Scotland which resulted in hospitalisation between 2021 and 2022.My officials will continue to work with relevant colleagues in the various agencies and departments in Northern Ireland and throughout the United Kingdom on this matter.  I will ensure where further scientific information is available on this subject of glycerol consumption that this is shared with the relevant groups in Northern Ireland and also with the wider public.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the clean-up from Storm Éowyn will be completed.
answer_text: The initial intensive phase of recovery following Storm Eowyn, which involved works to reopen the road network, including clearing fallen trees, has now finished. However, work to repair the many footways and roads damaged as a result of the storm remains ongoing and will be prioritised and actioned in line with available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the amount spent on the clean-up from Storm Éowyn to date; and (ii) the estimated total cost by time of completion.
answer_text: My Department has spent a total of £3.3m on the clean-up from Storm Éowyn to 28 February 2025, and the estimated total cost by the time of completion is expected to be in the region of £15.7m.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the time frame in which her Department should respond to planning permission applications.
answer_text: My department aims to make a recommendation on planning applications to myself within 30 weeks from the date of validation on regionally significant applications, within 30 weeks from date received for major called in applications, and within 15 weeks from date received on local called in applications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting times for paediatric clinical psychology services in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Northern Health Social Care Trust (NHSCT) have a Neonatal and Paediatric Clinical Psychology Service. The service covers the Neonatal Inpatient Unit, epilepsy, diabetes and complex health conditions only. The current waiting times for Clinical Psychology Service are 1 month for complex health conditions, 6 months for diabetes and 5 months for epilepsy. There is no waiting list for Neonatal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the current waiting times for paediatric cognitive behavioural therapy sessions.
answer_text: Children, young people and their families are not directly referred for paediatric cogitative behavioural therapy sessions and so there are no specific waiting times available. Young people requiring this type of intervention are instead referred to general paediatric clinical psychology services, where their needs are assessed before they are offered an appropriate therapeutic intervention. Services provided include individual based psychological assessment and neuropsychological assessments and a range of individual and group therapeutic interventions for children/young people and families. Some Trusts, such as the South Eastern also provide consultation, supervision, support and reflective practice as well as training; service development and research, service evaluations and auditLike many HSC services, Paediatric Clinical Psychology Services are under significant pressure, most notably due to increasing demand and workforce challenges. As a result, I am aware that individuals are having to wait longer than I would like to access provision, such as cogitative behavioural therapy. My department is currently leading several workstreams to address these wider pressures, most notably, through ongoing implementation of the strategy for Paediatric Healthcare Services, which is fully aligned with the priorities and objectives for the transformation of the Health and Social Care system as set out in the ‘Health And Wellbeing 2026: Delivering Together’.

To ask the Minister of Finance to outline how much his Department has spent on penalties from settling land tribunal cases.
answer_text: I understand, following clarification from the member, this question relates to the payment of interest by Land & Property Services (LPS) following a valuation appeal to the Lands Tribunal.   Where a refund of rates is due as a result, interest on that refund may be payable. Since 2009, the low Bank of England standard base rate has meant payment of interest has only become applicable since the start of the 2023/24 rating year. Since April 2023, LPS has not paid interest on any case appealed to the Lands Tribunal.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether any reviews have been carried out on the Victims’ Payments Scheme since its inception.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Board continue to work closely with victims’ groups to keep the Scheme under review and look for ways to improve the throughput of cases. We recently met with the President of the Board to hear directly from him on the progress of the Scheme, current challenges, and to reaffirm our continued support and commitment to the Scheme.The legislation required the Secretary of State to conduct a review of the operation the Scheme within 3 years of it opening for applications.  The Secretary of State published his Review in August 2024. The Review recognised that this is a complex Scheme to deliver with a range of challenges. It acknowledged the progress made by the Board in delivering the Scheme to date and highlighted the largely positive experiences of applicants who engaged with the Review Team.  The Review also included the decision by the Secretary of State to extend the backdating deadline to 30 August 2026, a point that we had written to the Secretary of State in support of, prior to the Review.  In addition, an independent “Real Time Review” was commissioned in the early stages to provide an assessment of arrangements within the Scheme to ensure that the applicant journey is victim centred.  The Board has recently had a review of its processes undertaken by Innovation and Consultancy Services. This has enabled the Board to transform and streamline its processes to offer improved services to applicants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the effectiveness of signage in forests in alerting members of the public to deer stalking, during appropriate seasons.
answer_text: AQO 1491/22-27I am satisfied that information signage, in respect of deer management practices on my Department’s Forest Service estate, is effective and complies with public health and safety risk management.  Forest Service display information signage during the deer culling season, from September until April, at entrances to the Department’s forests with high visitor numbers and large deer populations.  The signage is not a public control measure for peoples’ safety, rather it is intended to inform users, exercising their statutory right of pedestrian access, that they may encounter authorised personnel in the forest undertaking deer control activities and to follow the advice.Where sporting rights, including deer stalking, are exercised in a forest setting, there is no legal requirement for signage to be displayed. However, there is a requirement to meet health and safety obligations which includes managing any associated risks to the safety of visitors to the forest.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any exceptions to permit street lights on non-adopted streets or paths.
answer_text: Under my Department’s policy for the provision of street lighting, unadopted roads and pathways are generally not lit.However, in certain circumstances, my Department may consider providing street lighting in unadopted areas, provided agreement can be reached with the organisation or landowner responsible for the area.In this context, each request for lighting will be considered individually on its own merits and relevant factors would include the level of night-time usage, the proximity of alternative lit routes and other local factors.An example of where lighting may be installed in an unadopted area would be where a pathway or road provides pedestrian access between two locations with significant night-time usage, for example, to access public amenities and where there is no reasonable alternative lit route. A privately owned residential cul-de-sac would not normally be considered for lighting by my Department.I am conscious that street lighting can contribute significantly to feelings of personal security and safety, particularly for women travelling at night. This is also an important consideration when officials are considering lighting provision.My Officials are currently developing Active Travel proposals to improve pedestrian and cycling facilities to key destinations, with the aim of encouraging modal shift. Therefore, in certain circumstances, unadopted roads or footways that provide routes to Park & Ride sites or Railway Stations near towns, may also be considered for lighting.However, I would emphasise that the desire for lighting has to be balanced against environmental considerations, our desire to reduce the carbon footprint, electricity costs associated with our infrastructure and limited budgets.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what discussions they have had with their Executive colleagues in relation to the commitment in the draft Programme for Government 2024-2027 to cut health waiting times, given the current winter pressures.

answer_text: Work on the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations is ongoing. Stages 1 and 2 are complete, with 9 of the 20 proposals implemented in 2023 and 2024. My officials are continuing to work with the NIHE on the remaining proposals, which will be implemented in two stages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what impact Covid-19 has had on public health priorities in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what discussions he has had with his UK counterpart since the Westminster Government cut the Winter Fuel Payment.
answer_text: As I said when it was announced, restricting the Winter Fuel Payment to those in receipt of means tested benefits will have a detrimental impact on many in our society. I made clear to the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions, and other Ministers in the UK Government, my opposition to this decision and outlined the impact it will have on people in Northern Ireland.I met with Sir Stephen Timms MP, Minister of State for Social Security and Disability on 3 September, and also raised my concerns on the changes to the Winter Fuel Payment. I, along with Executive colleagues, have also written to the Prime Minister, Sir Keir Starmer, urging him to carry out an immediate review of the Pension Credit threshold. This must ensure that more low-income pensioner households, who are not financially secure and will narrowly miss out on a Winter Fuel Payment this year, can meet the eligibility criteria for Pension Credit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to work with GPs to return to pre-pandemic arrangements for more face-to-face appointments.
answer_text: In response to increasing patient demand GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care. There are approximately 200k consultation across general practice in Northern Ireland each per week, with around 50% of those delivered face to face.With regard to individual GP practice arrangements, GPs are independent contractors who provide primary medical services to their registered list of patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations.  As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.As part of supporting work in this area, my Department established a GP Access Working Group, which includes GP and service-user representation, to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients.  The GP Access Working Group is overseeing work to develop guidance for practices in the management of patient demand and optimising workflow within practices to maximise efficiency and improve experience for patients. The Group is also considering how technology can help to deliver improvements to the process for accessing services for patients and practices alike.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their plans to reform and transform public services, as outlined in the draft Programme for Government 2024-27.
answer_text: To help transform public services, a new Reform and Transformation Unit will be established. Work is already underway to scope out how such an office would operate across all departments, with a clear focus on the Executive’s priorities.It will be one part of a Reform Package which will include a Fiscal Framework, a Sustainability Plan, and a Transformation Programme.A central component of the package will also be a new Public Sector Transformation Board. This will involve a £235 million investment in an Executive Transformation Fund.A Sustainability Plan will be developed which will focus on looking at new approaches to planning, spending money well, funding vital public services, and delivering stable and sustainable public finances.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the timescales for establishing a Reform and Delivery Unit within the Executive Office.
answer_text: In the draft Programme for Government, we recognise the need for urgent Reform and Transformation to support and sustain the delivery of many of our public services. Reform and transformation cannot just be about money and how it is allocated. Instead, the focus needs to be on what we do, why we do it, how we do it, and being held to account for the results we achieve.To ensure we lay the foundations for this scale of change, we have committed to establishing a new Reform and Transformation Unit at the heart of government. Work is already underway to scope out how such an office would operate across Government and we will make further announcements in due course as this develops.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the eligibility criteria for the Basic Payment Scheme.
answer_text: To be eligible to claim payment under the Basic Payment Scheme a farmer must meet all of the following conditions: –They must hold at least 3 Basic Payment Scheme entitlements and have 3 ha of eligible agricultural land or are eligible to activate 3 Basic Payment Scheme entitlements by applying to the Regional Reserve.They must be farming the land that they are declaring (claiming) to activate entitlements.The land on which they claim payment must be at their disposal on 15 May in the year of the claim and remain eligible for the full calendar year.Any individual field they declare to activate Basic Payment Scheme entitlements must be at least 0.1 ha (except for common land).Note: By farming it is meant that they have the decision-making power, obtain the benefits and take the financial risks in relation to the agricultural activity on the land declared to activate entitlements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether landowners who are not active farmers are eligible to claim on the Basic Payment Scheme.
answer_text: Landowners who are not active farmers are not eligible for the Basic Payment Scheme.

answer_text: I submitted a bid for £60.8m for additional SEN funding at June monitoring.Whilst no additional funding was allocated specifically for SEN at June monitoring, a general allocation of £29.5m was received in respect of total resource bids submitted of £293.4m.From this general allocation, I allocated an additional £20m for SEN block grant pressures.Overall, I allocated the EA an additional £83.7m (includes £20m allocated in June Monitoring) for SEN in 2024-25 above its interim Budget allocation. Expenditure on SEN for 2024-25 is forecast to be c£622m. This has placed significant pressure on the Education Authority (EA) budget and the wider school system.I will continue to monitor projected financial requirements in respect of SEN and, wherever possible, bid for additional funding throughout the remainder of this financial year.Officials are currently finalising the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN).I hope to make an announcement to the Assembly setting out my plans for SEN transformation alongside a delivery plan shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what additional bids were made for policing from the Department of Justice in the last monitoring round.

Further to this, £18.7 million ringfenced resource DEL was sought for PSNI Depreciation pressures. As the member will be aware both the Resource and Capital bids in this monitoring round far outweighed the level of resources available. For resource funding we had 6 times as many demands with bids totalling some £1.27bn. Similarly for every £1 we have to spend on capital we had 9 times as many demands with bids in excess of £520m. The Executive has agreed to provide an additional £35 million of Resource DEL to the Department of Justice in the June Monitoring Round. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of Direct Assessment Units since their introduction.
answer_text: Direct Assessment Units provide same day acute care, meaning patients can be referred, diagnosed, treated and ideally discharged, all on the same day. These services offer an alternative pathway to the Emergency Departments, helping prevent unnecessary Emergency Department attendances and hospital admissions. Referrals to Direct Assessment Units can be made by: GPs, the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service and other community clinicians.Direct Assessment Units are operational in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust area, in both the Antrim and Causeway Hospital sites. The unit in Antrim was established in 2013 to deliver Same Day Acute Care to suitable ambulatory medical patients who would otherwise require to be admitted to hospital. The Causeway model has been in place since December 2018, providing a Frailty-only Direct Assessment Unit, delivering Same Day Acute Care to frail, elderly medical patients. The Units are Consultant led services bringing together staff from across various healthcare professions to ensure that patients get the best possible care.  As such, patients may be seen by staff from across a range of specialties such as: physiotherapy, occupational therapy, social work, pharmacy, dietetics, speech and language therapy, nursing and medicine.  The services provide diagnosis, observation and treatment on the same day as referral, meaning the vast majority of patients are discharged after their treatment, and therefore avoid a hospital admission.In 2023/24, my Department, through the Urgent and Emergency Care Review, invested recurrently in enhancements to these services.In Antrim, this funding will expand the existing Direct Assessment Unit footprint and enhance the service through the addition of ambulatory pathways for appropriate Cardiology and Respiratory conditions.The investment in Causeway has complemented the existing Frailty Direct Assessment Unit service by establishing an Acute Ambulatory Hub to allow Same Day Acute Care for other suitable medical and surgical patients. Further information for these new investments will be available from my Department, upon completion of the Post Project Evaluation process, that is currently ongoing in collaboration with Northern Health and Social Care Trust colleagues.Direct Assessment Units form just one part of the multi-disciplinary family of urgent care services that safely manage non-emergency demand, promote the scheduling of unscheduled care, and retain Emergency Departments for emergencies only. Other urgent care services include: Rapid Access Assessment and Treatment Services, Urgent Care Centres and Phone First Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department records the levels of convictions in relation to assaults on retail workers.
answer_text: Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not contain information on the occupation of assault victims, apart from where that is specified in the offence description, and there is no specific offence related to an assault on retail workers.  Attacks on retail workers may be prosecuted under more generic ‘assault’ type offence descriptions. Therefore, from information held, the Department is not able to provide the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will consider making the assault of a retail worker a standalone offence.
answer_text: Assault of a retail worker can be prosecuted under existing laws designed to protect any member of the public from harm. In light of increasing concerns about assaults on a range of workers who serve the public, I intend to introduce an offence of assaulting a person engaged in providing a service to the public or performing a public duty, with a higher maximum penalty in the magistrates’ courts than that currently available for the offence of common assault which applies regardless of the victim’s occupation. This would include retail workers.Alongside this new offence I intend to make assault on such a person in more serious cases a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be required to reflect that fact in their sentencing decisions.I intend to bring this new offence forward via my proposed Sentencing Bill before the end of this mandate.I am aware of the UK Government’s plans to make assaults on retail workers in England & Wales a standalone offence, though I note, under the current draft, the proposed new offence is to be triable only summarily.  The new offence I intend to introduce would be triable summarily or on indictment, and would therefore, afford a higher level of protection for retail workers, and other workers serving the public, than the offence planned for England & Wales.Following devolution of justice matters in 2010, authority for the development and introduction of criminal and civil offences and penalties was devolved to each Northern Ireland Assembly Department. It falls to the Department owning the overarching policy area to consider whether there are gaps in the law or amendments required to existing legislation and, where that is the case, responsibility for any subsequent legislative changes remains the responsibility of that Department. It would, therefore, be open to the Department of Economy to consider whether any specific provision is required in respect of the retail sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department's policy on bringing pedestrian steps up to an acceptable standard, where it has been identified that a person has suffered serious injury as a result of substandard steps.
answer_text: Article 8 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 places a duty on my Department to maintain all public roads in reasonable condition. We fulfil this duty by utilising a system of regular inspections of the road network and seeking to comply with a set of Maintenance Standards for Safety. When officials become aware of defects on pedestrian steps that are the responsibility of my Department, they will record the defect for repair in line with the guidance set out in Road Maintenance Standards for Safety and DEM 160/21 – Limited Service, both of which are available to view on my Department’s website.Although my Department is obliged to maintain its’ assets in a reasonable condition, there is no similar obligation to improve any aspect of the road asset or remedy any initial design / construction issues. The conversion of such steps to ramps may be included in a future programme of works for the area but this will depend on availability of funding and if the necessary gradient can be achieved.

To ask the Minister of Health how many children under 17 years old have Down Syndrome, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely collect data on children with Down Syndrome and to obtain this information by constituency would be at disproportionate cost. Therefore, we have requested this information from the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts who have provided the following responses. There are 180 under 17 year olds with Down Syndrome resident in the Northern HSC Trust, 159 in the Southern HSC Trust, and 130 in the Western HSC Trust. The Belfast and South Eastern HSC Trust have informed us that to obtain this information would require a manual trawl through patient files and therefore would be at disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people over 17 years old have Down Syndrome, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely collect data on people over 17 years old who have Down Syndrome and to obtain this information by constituency would be at disproportionate cost. Therefore, we have requested this information from the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts who have provided the following responses.The Northern HSC Trust has 223 known service users with Down Syndrome who are over 18 years old, the Southern HSC Trust has 149 known services users with Down Syndrome over 17 years old, and the Western HSC Trust has 259 known service users with Down Syndrome over 17 years old.The South Eastern HSC Trust could not provide this information as it would require a manual trawl though patient files at disproportionate cost.The Belfast HSC Trust have been unable to provide a response in the timeframe provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what changes her Department has made to the Disability Living Allowance application process, assessment and award for children with Down Syndrome, following the meeting that senior officials from Disability and Carers Service had with Families with Ups and Downs in December 2021.

Measures are in place to ensure that people claiming Disability Living Allowance are not required to undertake unnecessary reviews. These processes are subject to ongoing review and enhancement.

However, if the driver drives the minibus as an incidental part of their employment they would require a full minibus category D1 driving licence and Driver Qualification Card. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures and processes are in place for dispensing buprenorphine prescriptions.
answer_text: Buprenorphine is classed as an opioid analgesic and is indicated for the treatment of moderate to severe pain and also for the treatment of opioid dependence.Buprenorphine prescriptions can be dispensed by community pharmacies against either HS21 prescriptions issued by GP practices, or SP1/SP2 prescriptions issued by prescribers working in Trust Specialist Addiction Clinics.Treatment of Opioid Dependence.The prescriber will include the strength, quantity and directions for buprenorphine on the prescription, including a request to dispense in instalments (such as daily dispensing), if deemed appropriate. A community pharmacy that provides the Community Pharmacy Opioid Substitution Therapy (OST) Service will then dispense any buprenorphine prescriptions that they receive and label the medicine in accordance with the prescriber’s directions. The community pharmacy may also supervise the self-administration of buprenorphine in line with the prescriber’s directions.Moderate to Severe PainPrescriptions received for buprenorphine for the treatment of pain will be managed in the same way, i.e. the pharmacy will dispense and label the medicine in accordance with the prescriber’s directions.In all cases, any clinical queries will be followed up with the prescriber prior to dispensing. The patient will be counselled as needed and appropriate records of the dispensing will be maintained.The completed prescriptions are endorsed and submitted to the Business Services Organisation (BSO) on a monthly basis for payment to the community pharmacy, and to facilitate the monitoring of prescribing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many prescriptions for buprenorphine were issued in the last 12 months, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The HSC Business Services Organisation (BSO) holds data on prescriptions prescribed in primary care and dispensed by a community pharmacist. The number of prescriptions and items dispensed by constituency between 1st January 2021 and 31st December 2021 (in line with the latest available published Official Statistics) for buprenorphine are shown overleaf. Statistics for quarter 4 of the 2021/22 financial year will be available following publication of the General Pharmaceutical Statistics to quarter ending March 2022 on 30th June 2022.The number of prescriptions and items for buprenorphine1 dispensed, by constituency2, between 1st January 2021 & 31st December 2021 Constituency2Number of Prescription Forms Number of Items Belfast East14,83416,076 Belfast North14,38315,990 Belfast South9,30510,393 Belfast West 14,82216,015 East Antrim6,2516,745 East Londonderry9,20310,049 Fermanagh and South Tyrone11,67212,544 Foyle7,4888,143 Lagan Valley 11,09812,038 Mid Ulster8,4179,192 Newry and Armagh12,33313,200 North Antrim9,16710,094 North Down11,71912,420 South Antrim8,2618,813 South Down11,17811,866 Strangford11,74512,970 Upper Bann14,67915,460 West Tyrone10,01810,836 Unknown14,58427,247 Total211,157240,091 Source: Prescribing and dispensing information systems, Family Practitioner Services, HSC Business Services Organisation.1All information is for prescriptions prescribed in primary care and dispensed by a community pharmacist or dispensing doctor and based on the year of payment.  Data on secondary care prescribing and private prescriptions are not held by the Business Services Organisation, with the exception of specified controlled drugs including buprenorphine, which can be prescribed by consultants in a secondary care environment and dispensed in community pharmacies.2 Data is allocated to a constituency on the basis of the prescribing practice as assigned by the NISRA Central Postcode Directory. The constituency may therefore be different from the patient’s home address. Prescriptions categorised under ‘unknown’ do not have locational information and mainly refer to those prescribed by consultants in a secondary care environment and dispensed in community pharmacies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the window replacement scheme for Anderson Park and Anderson Court, Doagh, will commence.
answer_text: The Housing Executive is currently arranging for the windows to be inspected to determine whether their condition would merit replacement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the window replacement scheme will commence in The Steeple Estate, Antrim for those flats that were missed in previous schemes.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised me that there are 32 flats in the Steeple Estate included in scheme no 20781015 Antrim Double Glazing Parkhall Ward which is currently programmed for 2024/25. These flats are located in the following streets: Birch Hill Park; Central Park; Edenvale Park; and Seacash Drive. The Housing Executive’s Area Office is currently carrying out an exercise to ascertain if further addresses need to be added to this scheme.
